input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its main hardware layers consist of a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and autonomy elements (sensors, actuators, and control units). The vehicle operates as a testbed for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) in a controlled environment, providing sufficient fidelity for digital‑twin integration while being resource‑constrained at the ECU level.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) that displays real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and nearby object tracks, and enables users to view, save, and generate reports of ACC‑relevant data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed calculation based on inter‑vehicle distance) runs on the DT (e.g., cloud resources) and returns the desired speed to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI allows operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency‑brake commands remotely, supporting low‑latency bi‑directional communication. These services support two user classes: operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (troubleshooting via data collection).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware layer, enabling real‑time, low‑latency data exchange between vehicle and twin. Application modules are written in Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink, providing flexibility for algorithm development and integration. NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK is used to interface with GPU‑accelerated hardware on the twin side. Cloud infrastructure supplies the compute resources for off‑loading the ACC algorithm, while the communication stack employs DDS (via ROS2) to meet the safety‑critical latency requirements. All components are orchestrated within a digital twin system (DTS) that aggregates physical, middleware, and UI elements.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires device clock synchronization with a maximum error of 100 ms, mandated by hardware limitations and safety-critical vehicular standards. This synchronization granularity is applied to all computation‑offloading interactions between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture: software components are organized into three Functional Entities (FEs) – User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and Physical Twin Entity – each deployed on separate hardware. Every FE is realized as an individual ROS2 package containing one or more ROS2 nodes that implement the FE's functionality. The mapping of software components to FEs provides a clear orchestration layer, while the Physical Twin Entity hosts resource‑specific functions (e.g., sensors, actuators, point‑cloud clustering) and the Digital Twin Entity handles data management and control. This entity‑based decomposition dictates system‑wide coordination and resource allocation across the distributed hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking model that clusters raw LiDAR point clouds using the DBSCAN algorithm and subsequently tracks objects with a Kalman filter. An Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) logic model consumes the tracked object data and the ego vehicle state to generate speed set‑points. All generated object tracks and ego state records are persisted in a relational SQL database for historical analysis and replay. Data sources include LiDAR point clouds, depth‑camera outputs, and vehicle CAN bus telemetry. Model relationships are hierarchical: clustering feeds tracking, which together with ACC logic contributes to control decisions, while the SQL storage serves as the central data management layer.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the twin is realized through a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller driving a motor for longitudinal propulsion and a steering servo for lateral control. The software actuation component translates desired speed commands into motor voltage signals and generates steering angle commands for the servo. These actuators are addressable remotely via the Digital Twin interface, enabling closed‑loop control of vehicle speed and heading. Safety constraints and actuation limits are defined by the hardware specifications of the motor, servo, and ESC, though explicit limits are not detailed in the source.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Physical Twin is equipped with onboard sensors comprising a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR and an Intel Deepsense D435i depth camera. The LiDAR provides 2‑D distance measurements for obstacle detection, while the depth camera supplies synchronized RGB‑D streams for scene understanding. Sensor data are streamed continuously and fused in real time within the Twin. Specific sampling frequencies, transmission rates, and accuracy metrics are not enumerated, but the sensors collectively furnish the raw data for the clustering and tracking models.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data originating from the Physical Twin (LiDAR point clouds, motor status, steering servo position, vehicle state) is first ingested by the Data Pre‑processing FE. The raw LiDAR point cloud is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which converts the unordered point set into tracked object representations. All data streams then pass through the Data Translation FE (ISO 23247 Data Translation FE) where each stream is serialized, message types are aligned, and unit conversions are performed to create a common digital format. Within the Digital Twin System (DTS) the processed data are exchanged between software components over a ROS 2 network (DDS‑based middleware). Communication between entities (Physical Twin ↔ Digital Twin) uses a Wi‑Fi network employing UDP at the transport layer. The flow is event‑driven by the need to display ACC‑relevant information (vehicle state, object tracks, projected path) on the real‑time 3D visualization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate from the User Interface FE (Control Panel) and are routed through the Controlling FE (ACC) to the Actuation FE. The Actuation FE translates high‑level commands such as desired speed, steering angle or braking request into motor voltage or servo position commands, which are sent to the physical vehicle actuators. Command messages are formatted according to the ROS 2 data model and are passed through the Data Translation FE to ensure correct units and message alignment before reaching the physical actuators. Execution confirmation is provided by feedback streams of actuator status that are fed back into the monitoring pipeline.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin System uses a two‑layer network architecture: intra‑entity communication is realized with ROS 2 (DDS‑based) on the local computational platform, and inter‑entity communication employs a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. The system is designed to satisfy a maximum end‑to‑end latency of 100 ms (Requirement R7). Reliability is supported by ROS 2’s built‑in QoS policies; redundancy is achieved by deploying each functional entity on separate hardware components. Security is confined to the network layer, with Wi‑Fi providing link‑level protection; no additional security protocols are described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Each functional entity of the DTS (Data Monitoring, Actuation, Control, Visualization, etc.) is deployed on dedicated hardware components, allowing a distributed edge deployment of the twin. Persistent vehicle state data are stored on a storage device external to the vehicle (Requirement R3). The deployment model supports on‑premise edge nodes with the possibility of cloud integration for long‑term storage and analytics, and includes scalability by adding further hardware nodes for additional sensor or processing workloads. Availability is addressed by the separation of entities across hardware, which isolates failures and maintains system operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering approach follows ISO 23247 Part 2 reference architecture. Development proceeds by first identifying the key software components required for the Digital Twin, then mapping each component onto a Functional Entity (FE) that is responsible for a concrete task such as actuation, data transformation, or user interfacing. The standard specifies the entity‑based structure (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) but does not prescribe concrete tools for version control, testing, or quality assurance. Implementations may employ OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for the communication layer between entities.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin conforms to the ISO 23247 series (published 2021), specifically leveraging Part 2 (Reference Architecture) which defines an entity‑based architecture composed of Observable Manufacturing Elements, Data Collection and Device Control Entities, Digital Twin Entities, and User Entities, each subdivided into Functional Entities. The implementation may employ OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for data collection, device control, and interoperability, aligning with the information‑exchange principles of Part 4 of ISO 23247.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the description of the physical twin in the source text, detailing the scale, chassis layers, autonomy components, and the specific resource constraints of the ECU. It correctly identifies the vehicle's role as a testbed for ACC. Quotes: 'teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle', 'lower level chassis... upper level chassis... autonomy elements', 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU)'.

The description perfectly matches the three services defined in Section III.A of the text: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. It captures specific details such as UI functionalities, the offloading of ACC computation, and remote control commands. Quotes: 'Data Monitoring: Monitoring ACC-relevant data', 'Computation Offloading: offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'Remote Management: enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed'.

The extraction correctly identifies Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware for real-time, low-latency data exchange, as stated in the source: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) [32] as the middleware to integrate application modules'. It accurately lists the programming languages Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink, matching the source's mention of 'Python 3.8 (rapid prototyping), C++ (ROS2 node lifecycle management), and MATLAB/Simulink'. NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK is correctly noted for interfacing with GPU-accelerated hardware, as the source states: 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for programming the Jetson Orin Nano'. Cloud infrastructure for offloading ACC computation is mentioned, aligning with the source's discussion of offloading to 'better-suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud'. The use of DDS via ROS2 for safety-critical latency is implied but not explicitly stated, though the source mentions 'low end-to-end latency communication' and ROS2's use of DDS is well-known but not in the provided text. The overall description is highly specific and well-grounded in the sources.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the 100 ms synchronization requirement and its link to computation offloading and hardware limitations. However, it slightly misattributes the causality of the requirement; the text states that standards mandate *stricter* (lower) values than 100 ms, while the specific 100 ms value is required due to 'hardware limitations'. Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations', and 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time'.

The document describes the development of a single Digital Twin for a single 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs. decentralized architectures involving multiple twins, or hierarchical structures of twins. Therefore, the 'Not in Document' assessment is correct.

The extraction is highly detailed and accurately reflects the system's architecture based on ISO 23247. It correctly describes the three entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin), their deployment on separate hardware, their implementation as ROS2 packages, and the specific functions handled by the Digital and Physical Twin Entities. Supported by quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA', 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', and 'we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package'.

The text describes internal communication within the Digital Twin System (DTS) using ROS2 and Wi-Fi but does not detail integration with external systems, interoperability standards, or other enterprise systems/Digital Twins. It mentions 'offloading... to the cloud' as a capability but does not describe external integration patterns or protocols. Thus, 'Not in Document' is a correct assessment.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

The description is highly specific and well-supported by the text. It correctly identifies the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', the conversion of 'desired speed' into 'motor voltage commands', and the remote management capabilities via the UI. The note regarding the absence of explicit actuation limits is also accurate.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific sensor models ('Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera') and their role in object detection and data fusion. However, it includes technical details not present in the source text, specifically describing the LiDAR as '2‑D' and the camera output as 'RGB‑D streams', which relies on outside knowledge of the hardware.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The text focuses on architectural design and requirements rather than specific model fidelity analysis or validation methodologies. While it mentions general definitions like 'real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections' and system requirements such as 'maintain network latency of at most 100 ms', it lacks the specific details requested regarding 'validation and verification methods, uncertainty sources and quantification, [or] error detection and correction mechanisms'.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_

Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in

ENGINEERING A DT FOR ADAPTIVE CRUISE CONTROL'; 'DTs are also used as a product lifecycle management tool... establishing a continuous, end-to-end information flow throughout the lifecycle' (mentioned only in related work).

The extraction provides a highly specific and accurate description of the engineering process found in Section IV.B, which states: 'First, we identify key software (SW) components... Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement them.' It also correctly summarizes the standard's limitations regarding implementation guidelines, citing that 'The standard does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented' (Section II.A).
